title: Lesson 1 — The Debate Club

words.definition: the quality of being open, honest, and frank in expression
words.example: She answered the interviewer's toughest questions with surprising candor.

words.definition: departing from what is normal, usual, or expected; atypical
words.example: A single aberrant reading made the scientists recheck the whole experiment.

words.definition: excessively or pointlessly talkative, especially about trivial matters
words.example: The garrulous passenger narrated his entire life story before we left the runway.

words.definition: using very few words; terse to the point of seeming rude or mysterious
words.example: His laconic reply, "Fine," told us nothing about the meeting.

words.definition: acting with or showing care and sensible forethought
words.example: It was prudent to save part of every paycheck before the layoffs began.

words.definition: given to sudden, unpredictable changes of mood or behavior
words.example: The capricious weather swung from sun to sleet within an hour.

words.definition: severe or plain in manner or appearance; without comfort or adornment
words.example: The monastery's austere rooms held nothing but a cot and a candle.

words.definition: fond of the company of others; sociable
words.example: So gregarious was she that she knew every neighbor within a week of moving in.

words.definition: just begun and so not fully formed or developed; rudimentary
words.example: The plan was still inchoate, more a hunch than a strategy.
The perfidious ally secretly sold the battle plans to the enemy.

words.definition: lasting for a very short time; fleeting
words.example: Fame on social media can be ephemeral, forgotten within a day.

words.definition: dealing with things sensibly and realistically rather than idealistically
words.example: Her pragmatic solution ignored the theory and simply fixed the leak.

words.definition: to regard with great respect or reverence
words.example: Students came from across the country to venerate the aging scholar.

words.definition: having mixed, contradictory feelings about something
words.example: He felt ambivalent about the promotion, proud yet dreading the longer hours.
The evidence linking the two events was tenuous at best.

story: When Mara joined the debate club, its new advisor greeted her with disarming [[candor|candor]]: "Most of you will quit by October." Such [[aberrant|aberrant]] bluntness startled the [[garrulous|garrulous]] seniors, who could talk for hours about nothing, but Mara — always [[laconic|laconic]] — simply nodded. The advisor was a [[prudent|prudent]] planner, yet oddly [[capricious|capricious]] in taste: one week he demanded [[austere|austere]], stripped-down speeches, the next he wanted theatrical flourish. Though [[gregarious|gregarious]] and quick to gather a crowd, he distrusted any argument that was still [[inchoate|inchoate]], only half-formed in a student's mind. He warned the club about a [[perfidious|perfidious]] rival coach who had once leaked their notes, and reminded them that applause is [[ephemeral|ephemeral]], gone by morning. His advice stayed relentlessly [[pragmatic|pragmatic]]: win the judge, not the room. In time the younger members came to [[venerate|venerate]] him, even while they felt [[ambivalent|ambivalent]] about his blunt methods. By spring, Mara's once-[[tenuous|tenuous]] grip on her nerves had hardened into real confidence, and she realized his grim prophecy had, for her at least, been gloriously wrong.
